elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that if $d \ge 2$ is an integer, $G$ is a finite abelian group, $Z_0$ is a subset of $G$ not contained in any strict coset in $G$, and $E_1,\dots,E_d$ are dense subsets of $G^n$ such that the sumset $E_1+\dots+E_d$ avoids $Z_0^n$ then $E_1, \dots, E_d$ essentially have bounded dimension.
elements.sent-2411-002: More precisely, they are almost entirely contained in sets $E_1' \times G^{I^c , \dots, E_d' \times G^{I^c}$, where the size of $I \subset [n]$ is non-zero and independent of $n$, and $E_1',\dots,E_d'$ are subsets of $G^{I}$ such that the sumset $E_1'+\dots+E_d'$ avoids $Z_0^I$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: An important direction in combinatorial number theory and geometry considers questions that are broadly of the following kind:~given two subsets $A$,$B$ of some ambient (abelian) group, what may be deduced about the structure of $A$,$B$ if the sumset $A+B$ is somehow constrained? Among various constraints that may be considered for $A+B$, the most basic and most studied is bounding $|A+B|$ in terms of $|A|$ and $|B|$.
elements.sent-2411-004: For example, when $A=B$, there is a long line of research describing the structure of finite sets $A \subset \Z$ with $|A+A| \le K|A|$ for some fixed $K$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Here the landmark result is Freiman's theorem, which shows that such $A$ must be contained in some multidimensional arithmetic progression $P$ with $\dim(P)$ and $|P|/|A|$ both bounded by a constant depending only on $K$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Further milestones in this direction are an extension to general (not necessarily abelian) groups by Breuillard, Green and Tao \cite{Breuillard Green Tao and a resolution of Marton's Conjecture (aka the Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa Conjecture) on a polynomial quantitative improvement for abelian groups with bounded torsion by Gowers, Green, Manners and Tao \cite{Gowers Green Manners Tao .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: It is also natural to constrain $A+B$ not by placing an upper bound on its size, but by requiring that it avoids a specific structured set.
elements.sent-2411-008: For instance, a theorem of S\'ark\"ozy \cite{Sarkozy provides upper bounds on the size of a subset $A \subset [n]$ such that $A-A$ does not contain any prime integer, and Green \cite{Green recently obtained a related result for shifted primes.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Another well-studied example is that of subsets $A \subset [n]$ such that $A-A$ does not contain any perfect square, for which bounds were obtained by S\'ark\"ozy \cite{Sarkozy and Furstenberg \cite{Furstenberg , with a more recent improvement by Bloom and Maynard \cite{Bloom Maynard .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Our own focus in the present paper will be on high-dimensional subsets $E,F$ of a large power $G^n$ of some fixed finite abelian group $G$, satisfying the constraint that for some fixed $Z_0 \subset G$ the sumset $E+F$ avoids the power $Z_0^n$.
elements.sent-2411-011: To illustrate this, we consider two subsets $A,B$ of $\Z^n$ with constraints according to residues modulo some integer $N$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Here we give two examples, the first being trivial, and the second already capturing the main difficulties of the question.
elements.sent-2411-013: Suppose first that $N=2$ and we require that there is no $(a,b) \in A \times B$ with $a_i+b_i$ even for every $i \in [n]$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Equivalently, projecting $A,B$ modulo $2$ to subsets $E,F$ of $\Z_2^n$, we require that $E+F$ does not contain $\{0\}^n \in \Z_2^n$, or equivalently that $F$ is disjoint from $-E$.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this example, we do not obtain any non-trivial structure for $E$ and $F$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Equivalently, projecting $A,B$ modulo $N$ to subsets $E,F$ of $\Z_N^n$, we require $E+F$ to be disjoint from $\{0,1\}^n$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Here it is not clear what structure this imposes on $E$ and $F$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, one may hope that an answer to this question may lead to progress on the longstanding Additive Basis Conjecture of Jaeger, Linial, Payan and Tarsi (reported by Alon, Linial and Meshulam \cite{Alon Linial Meshulam ), which is equivalent to the statement that for any prime $p$ there is a constant $C=C(p)$ such that for any invertible linear maps $A_i \in GL(n,\Z_p)$ for $1 \le i \le C$ we have $A_1(\{0,1\}^n) + \cdots A_C(\{0,1\}^n) = \Z_p^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In a companion paper \cite{Karam Keevash we solve the corresponding continuous extremal problem for $E$ and $F$ in the torus $(\R/\Z)^n$, which can be rephrased in terms of extremal expansions by cubes: given $m, \ell \in (0,1)$ we describe the extremal examples (and show stability) for minimising $\mu(E + [0,\ell]^n)$ among all $E \subset (\R/\Z)^n$ with $\mu(E)=m$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: One application of our extremal result is another proof of the known bound $C < O(\log n)$ for the Additive Basis Conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-022: The present paper concerns structural properties of $E,F$ with $(E+F) \cap \{0,1\}^n = \emptyset$ that have density bounded below but may be quite far from any extremal example.
elements.sent-2411-023: Next we make three basic observations that motivate our structural result for subsets $E,F$ of $\Z_N^n$ such that $E+F$ avoids $\{0,1\}^n$ (or more generally $Z_0^n$ for some $Z_0 \subset \Z_N$).
elements.sent-2411-024: Many natural examples are low-dimensional, e.g. $E=F= \{x \in \Z_3^n: x_1 = 1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Any example in some dimension can be extended to any higher dimension: if $E',F'$ are subsets of $\Z_N^k$ such that $E'+F'$ avoids $\{0,1\}^k$ then we can extend $E',F'$ to subsets $E,F$ of $\Z_N^n$ for any $n>k$ simply by taking $E = E' \times \Z_N^{n-k}$ and $F = F' \times \Z_N^{n-k}$.
elements.sent-2411-026: If $E+F$ avoids $\{0,1\}^n$ then so does $E'+F'$ for any $E' \subset E$ and $F' \subset F$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Conversely, our main result will state that any $E,F$ with $(E+F) \cap Z_0^n$ empty can be approximated by sets obtained by these three moves (starting with a low-dimensional set, extending to a higher dimension, taking arbitrary subsets).
elements.sent-2411-028: We start with the formulation when $N$ is prime and $E+F$ avoids $Z_0^n$ for some $Z_0 \subset \Z_N$ with $|Z_0|>1$ (as illustrated above, if $|Z_0|=1$ then $E,F$ need not have any non-trivial structure).
elements.sent-2411-029: Throughout, if $I$ is a subset of $[n]$ we write $I^c$ for the complement of $I$ in $[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Let $p$ be a prime, let $\e > 0$, and let $Z_0 \subset \Z_p$ with $|Z_0|>1$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Then there exists $C=C(p,\e)$ such that for any subsets $E,F$ of $\Z_p^n$ with $(E+F) \cap Z_0^n = \emptyset$ there exist $I \subset [n]$ with $0<|I|<C$ and subsets $E',F'$ of $\Z_p^{I}$ satisfying \begin{equation}|E \setminus (E' \times \Z_p^{I^c})| \le \e |\Z_p^n|, \quad |F \setminus (F' \times \Z_p^{I^c})| \le \e |\Z_p^n|, \quad (E'+F') \cap Z_0^I = \emptyset.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: We note that in Theorem \ref{Main result we require the set $I$ of structured coordinates to have bounded size, be non-empty (for non-triviality) and be the same for both $E$ and $F$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: If instead we showed that $E$ and $F$ are structured with respect to some unrelated sets $I_E$ and $I_F$ then we could recover the formulation above by taking $I = I_E \cup I_F$, but in the converse direction we cannot allow unrelated sets (e.g.~if $I_E \cap I_F = \emptyset$ then we may have $E+F=\Z_p^n$).
elements.sent-2411-034: Next we suppose that the modulus $N$ is not necessarily prime.
elements.sent-2411-035: Compared to the case of cyclic groups $\Z_N$ it will cost us little to tackle the case of finite abelian groups, so we shall present the corresponding generalisation of Theorem \ref{Main result in the latter setting.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Throughout, if $G$ is a finite abelian group, then we will say that a strict coset in $G$ is a set of the type $H + \{x\}$ where $H$ is a subgroup of $G$ with $H \ne G$, and $x$ is an element of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Let $G$ be a finite abelian group and let $\e > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Then there exists $C=C(G,\e)$ such that if $Z_0 \subset G$ is not contained in any strict coset in $G$ and $E,F$ are subsets of $G^n$ with $(E+F) \cap Z_0^n = \emptyset$ then there exist $I \subset [n]$ with $0<|I|<C$ and subsets $E',F'$ of $G^{I}$ satisfying \begin{equation}|E \setminus (E' \times G^{I^c})| \le \e |G^n|, \quad |F \setminus (F' \times G^{I^c})| \le \e |G^n|, \quad (E'+F') \cap Z_0^I = \emptyset.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-039: The assumption on $Z_0$ cannot be weakened, as if $Z_0$ is contained in a coset of some strict subgroup $H$ of $G$ then under the projection $\pi:G \to G/H$ we have $|\pi(Z_0)| \le 1$, which leads to a lack of structure similarly to the case $|Z_0|=1$ (see Example \ref{counterexample for two sets for details).}
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, we give a further extension of Theorem \ref{Main result, groups case that considers several summands.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Let $d \ge 2$ be an integer, let $G$ be a finite abelian group, and let $\e > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Then there exists $C=C(d,G,\e)$ such that if $Z_0 \subset G$ is not contained in any strict coset in $G$ and $E_1,\dots,E_d$ are subsets of $G^n$ with $(E_1+\dots+E_d) \cap Z_0^n = \emptyset$ then there exist $I \subset [n]$ with $0<|I|<C$ and subsets $E_1',\dots,E_d'$ of $G^{I}$ satisfying \begin{equation}|E_j \setminus (E_j' \times G^{I^c})| \le \e |G^n|  for all  j \in [d], \quad (E_1'+\dots+E_d') \cap Z_0^I = \emptyset.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section \ref{Section: The case of two subsets of F_p^n we will prove Theorem \ref{Main result .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section \ref{Section: Extending to several subsets of finite abelian groups discusses the necessary modifications to prove its two successive generalisations, Theorem \ref{Main result, groups case and Theorem \ref{Main result, groups case, several sets .}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The final Section \ref{Section: Further discussion contains some potential further generalisations and remaining open questions.}
elements.sent-2411-046: If $X$ is a finite set, $I$ is a subset of $[n]$, and $E$ is a subset of $X^I$, then we refer to the ratio $|E|/|X^I|$ as the density of $E$ inside $X^I$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Usually there will be no ambiguity as to which set $X^I$ is being considered and we will denote this density by $d(E)$.
elements.sent-2411-048: If $y$ is an element of $X^I$ and $E$ is a subset of $X^n$, then we write $E_{I \to y}$ for the set of points $x \in E$ such that $x_i = y_i$ for every $i \in I$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Although the set $E_{I \to y}$ is a subset of $\Z_p^n$ rather than a subset of $\Z_p^{I^c}$, we can also view it as a subset of $\Z_p^{I^c}$, and the notation $d(E_{I \to y )$ will always refer to the density of $E_{I \to y}$ as a subset of $\Z_p^{I^c}$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: If $I,J$ are disjoint subsets of $[n]$, $y$ and $z$ are elements of $X^I$ and $X^J$ respectively, and $E$ is a subset of $\Z_p^n$, then we write $E_{I \to y, J \to z}$ for the set $E_{I \cup J, w}$ where $w$ is the element of $X^{I \cup J}$ defined by $w_i = y_i$ for each $i \in I$ and $w_i = z_i$ for each $i \in J$.
elements.sent-2411-051: In the present section we prove Theorem \ref{Main result .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The two main technical ingredients of the proof are (i) a simultaneous regularity lemma for two sets, and (ii) a result of Ha\k{z}\l{ a, Holenstein and Mossel on product space models of correlation.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We present the first ingredient in the first subsection, which is a minor modification of an existing result, although we include a proof for the convenience of the reader.
elements.sent-2411-054: In the second subsection we prove Theorem \ref{Main result assuming a lemma on summands in dense pseudorandom sets.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We then conclude the proof in the third subsection by using the second ingredient mentioned above to prove this lemma.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Pseudorandom sets and a simultaneous regularity lemma
elements.sent-2411-056: In our context, a regularity lemma is a result on decomposing any subset of a product set $X^n$ into a bounded number of pieces, most of which are pseudorandom, in the following sense.
elements.sent-2411-057: Let $X$ be a finite set, let $r$ be a nonnegative integer and let $\b>0$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We say that a subset $E$ of $X^n$ is $(r,\b)$-pseudorandom if for any subset $I$ of $[n]$ with size at most $r$ and every $y \in X^I$ we have $|d(E_{I \to y ) - d(E)| \le \b$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The following formulation is similar to that in \cite[Lemma 3.2]{Keevash Lifshitz Long Minzer , with the slight complication of simultaneously decomposing two sets; the required modification of the proof is straightforward, but we give the details for the convenience of the reader.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Let $X$ be a finite set, let $r$ be a nonnegative integer, and let $\b,\a>0$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Then there exists $C = \mathrm{Psr _2(X,r,\b,\a)$ such that for any subsets $E,F$ of $X^n$ there exists $I \subset [n]$ with $0<|I|<C$ which simultaneously satisfies \begin{equation}\P_{y \in X^I} (E_{I \to y}  not  (r,\b) -pseudorandom) \le \a, \label{first pseudorandomness inequality} \P_{y \in X^I} (F_{I \to y}  not  (r,\b) -pseudorandom) \le \a. \label{second pseudorandomness inequality}\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\P_{y \in X^I} (E_{I \to y}  not  (r,\b) -pseudorandom) \le \a, \label{first pseudorandomness inequality} \P_{y \in X^I} (F_{I \to y}  not  (r,\b) -pseudorandom) \le \a. \label{second pseudorandomness inequality}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: We construct the set $I$ using an inductive process.
elements.sent-2411-063: Let us begin by ignoring the requirement that $I$ is non-empty (which will be easy to also ensure).
elements.sent-2411-064: For a positive integer $s$, at the $s$th iteration we proceed as follows, unless we have stopped before then.
elements.sent-2411-065: Having obtained a set $I_s$, if the inequalities \eqref{first pseudorandomness inequality and \eqref{second pseudorandomness inequality hold for $I=I_s$ then we stop.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Let us now assume that \eqref{first pseudorandomness inequality fails (if instead \eqref{second pseudorandomness inequality fails, then we proceed similarly with $E$ replaced by $F$).}}
elements.sent-2411-067: We then define the set \begin{equation}E_s^{psr}= \{y \in X^{I_s}: E_{I_s \to y}  not  (r,\b) -pseudorandom \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: For each $y \in E_s^{\mathrm{psr }$ we can find some $I_{s+1,y \subset I_s^c$ with size at most $r$ satisfying \begin{equation}|d(E_{I_s \to y, I_{s+1,y} \to z}) - d(E_{I_s \to y})| > \b\end{equation} for some $z \in I_{s+1,y}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: To complete the inductive step we set \begin{equation}I_{s+1} = I_s \cup \bigcup_{y \in E_s^{psr}} I_{s+1,y}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: We next show that the process terminates after a number of iterations that is bounded above depending on $p,r,\b,\a$ only.
elements.sent-2411-071: We will do so by an energy-increment argument.
elements.sent-2411-072: For each step $s$ of the induction we define the energies \begin{equation}\ce_s(E) = \E_{y \in X^{I_s}} d(E_{I_s \to y})^2, \qquad \ce_s(F) = \E_{y \in X^{I_s}} d(F_{I_s \to y})^2.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\ce_s(E) = \E_{y \in X^{I_s}} d(E_{I_s \to y})^2, \qquad \ce_s(F) = \E_{y \in X^{I_s}} d(F_{I_s \to y})^2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: The sets $I_s$ constitute an increasing sequence (with respect to inclusion), so the sequences $\ce_s(E)$ and $\ce_s(F)$ are nondecreasing by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.
elements.sent-2411-074: If some step $s$ of the induction involves $E$, then we may lower bound the difference \begin{equation}\ce_{s+1}(E) - \ce_{s}(E) = \E_{y \in X^{I_s}} \left( \E_{z \in X^{I_{s+1} \setminus I_s}} d(E_{I_s \to y, I_{s+1} \setminus I_s \to z})^2 - d(F_{I_s \to y})^2 \right)\end{equation} by interpreting for every $y \in X^{I_s}$ the inner expectation as the variance of the variable $d(E_{I_s \to y, I_{s+1 \setminus I_s \to z )$ when $z$ is chosen uniformly at random in $X^{I_{s+1 \setminus I_s}$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\ce_{s+1}(E) - \ce_{s}(E) = \E_{y \in X^{I_s}} \left( \E_{z \in X^{I_{s+1} \setminus I_s}} d(E_{I_s \to y, I_{s+1} \setminus I_s \to z})^2 - d(F_{I_s \to y})^2 \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: For every $y \in E_s^{\mathrm{psr }$, the variance of the variable $d(E_{I_s \to y, I_{s+1 \setminus I_s \to z )$ when $z$ is chosen uniformly at random in $X^{I_{s+1 \setminus I_s}$ is again (by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality) at least the variance of the variable $d(E_{I_s \to y, I_{s+1,y \to z )$ when $z$ is chosen uniformly at random in $X^{I_{s+1,y }$, which is always at least $p^{-r \b^2$ by definition of $E_s^{\mathrm{psr }$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Hence we obtain the lower bound \begin{equation}\ce_{s+1}(E) - \ce_{s}(E) \ge p^{-r} \a \b^2,\end{equation} which is independent of $s$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Thus the sum of the energies $\ce_{s (E) + \ce_{s (F)$ increases by at least $p^{-r \a \b^2$ at each step, and since it is always bounded above by $2$, the number of steps is at most $2 p^r \a^{-1 \b^{-2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: At every step, the set $I_{s+1 \setminus I_s$ has size bounded above by $p^{|I_s| r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: To ensure that $I$ is non-empty, at the very first iteration we continue (rather than stop) regardless of whether \eqref{first pseudorandomness inequality or \eqref{second pseudorandomness inequality holds, and for that iteration the difference $(\ce_1(E) + \ce_1(F)) - (\ce_0(E) + \ce_0(F))$ is lower bounded by $0$ rather than by $p^{-r \a \b^2$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-102: Let $(U,V)$ be random variables following some probability distribution $\mathcal{P}$ on $\Omega^2$, where $\Omega$ is some finite set.
elements.sent-2411-103: The correlation of $\mathcal{P$} is \begin{equation}\rho(P)= \sup\{\Cov(\ll(U),\s(V)) : \Var \ll(U) = \Var \s(V) = 1 \},\end{equation} where the supremum is over functions $\ll,\s: \Omega \to \R$ with $\Var \ll(U) = \Var \s(V) = 1$.

elements.sent-2411-105: Let $\Omega$ be a finite set and $(U,V)$ be random variables following some probability distribution $\mathcal{P}$ on $\Omega^2$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Then $\rho(\cp) = 1$ if and only if there exist non-constant $\ll,\s: \Omega \to \R$ such that $\ll(U)=\s(V)$ with probability $1$.
elements.sent-2411-107: Since the quantities $\Cov(\ll(U),\s(V)), \Var \ll(U), \Var \s(V)$ are unchanged after subtracting constants from $\ll$ and $\s$, we can view $\rho(\cp)$ as the supremum of $\Cov(\ll(U),\s(V))$ over all $\ll,\s$ with expectation $0$ and variance $1$.
elements.sent-2411-108: This supremum is a maximum, as the set of such pairs $(\ll,\s)$ is compact (since the set $\Omega$ is finite).
elements.sent-2411-109: We consider $(\ll,\s)$ attaining the maximum and apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality \begin{equation}\rho(\cp) = \Cov(\ll(U),\s(V))^2 \le \Var \ll(U) \Var \s(V) = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-110: If $\rho(\cp) = 1$ then we have equality in this inequality, so $\ll(U)=\s(V)$ with probability $1$; this gives the required conclusion, as $\ll,\s$ are non-constant (they have non-zero variance).

elements.sent-2411-128: In the present section we will generalise our result successively from cyclic groups of prime order to finite abelian groups and then from two summands to several summands.
elements.sent-2411-129: The main ideas of the proofs will be the same, although some non-trivial modifications are needed, and the second generalisation will require more of the framework developed in \cite{Hazla Holenstein Mossel .}
elements.sent-2411-130: We begin with an extension of Theorem \ref{Main result replacing $\Z_p$ for $p$ prime by any finite abelian group $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-131: As mentioned in the introduction, we will need to strengthen our assumption on $Z_0$ to not being contained in any strict coset in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-132: The following construction shows that this assumption on $Z_0$ is necessary for us to obtain any non-trivial structure for $E,F$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Let $G$ be a finite abelian group and let $Z_0 \subset H + \{x\}$, where $H$ is a strict subgroup of $G$ and $x \in G$.
elements.sent-2411-134: Let $K$ be the quotient group $G/H$ and let $\pi:G \to K$ be the projection from $G$ to $K$.
elements.sent-2411-136: Let $E_K$,$F_K$ be two subsets of $K^n$ such that for any $x_K \in E_K$ and $y_K \in F_K$ we have $x_K+y_K \neq \kk$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Then for any $x \in E$ and $y \in F$ we have $\pi^{\otimes n (x)+\pi^{\otimes n (y) \in E_K + F_K$, so $x+y \notin Z_0^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: However, the sets $E_K$,$F_K$ are fairly arbitrary: e.g.~if $\kk=0$ then the only condition is that $E_K$ and $-F_K$ are disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-140: Now we modify the proof of Theorem \ref{Main result to prove Theorem \ref{Main result, groups case .}}

elements.sent-2411-146: Thus for any $y_1, y_2 \in Z_0$ we have $\s(y_1-x) = \ll(x) = \s(y_2-x)$ for every $x \in G$.
elements.sent-2411-147: Equivalently, $\s(x) = \s(x+y)$ for all $x \in G$ and all $y \in Z_0-Z_0$.
elements.sent-2411-148: Taking $x=0$ and iterating this identity, we see that $\s$ is constant on the subgroup $H$ generated by $Z_0-Z_0$.
elements.sent-2411-149: Furthermore, $Z_0$ is contained in a coset of $H$, as for any $x \in Z_0$ we have $Z_0-\{x\} \subset Z_0 - Z_0 \subset H$.
elements.sent-2411-150: By assumption on $Z_0$, we therefore have $H=G$, so $\s$ is constant.

elements.sent-2411-223: For further insight on the problem for general functions $f$, it seems natural to generalise from addition in abelian groups, to multiplication in general groups, and then further to latin squares, namely functions $f: X \times X \to X$ (for some finite set $X$) such that for every $x,y$ in $X$ there is a unique $z$ such that $f(x,z)=y$ and a unique $z'$ such that $f(z',x)=y$.
